---
title: "Student Mental Health & Study Habits Analysis"
subtitle: "ETC5513 Assignment 3"
author: "Team Members 1-4"
date: "2026-05"
---

# Executive Summary

Student mental health has emerged as a critical concern across university
campuses globally [@hunt2010mental]. This study analyses survey data from
1,200 students to examine how sleep quality, study habits, and counseling
access relate to stress and anxiety outcomes. Students reporting good sleep
quality showed stress levels 3.1 points lower than poor sleepers, and those
with counseling access reported 2.1 points lower anxiety scores. These
findings highlight sleep optimisation and expanded counseling services as
practical, evidence-based interventions for improving student wellbeing
[@walker2017sleep].

# Introduction

Student mental health has become a growing priority for universities
worldwide. Academic pressure, financial stress, and social adjustment
create significant psychological burden for many students. Research
consistently shows that 60-70% of university students experience moderate
to severe stress during their studies [@gaultney2010prevalence]. Poor
mental health is associated with lower academic performance, higher dropout
rates, and long-term health consequences. Daily lifestyle habits including
sleep, exercise, and study patterns play an important role in shaping
mental health outcomes. However, most existing research focuses on general
adult populations rather than student-specific contexts. Understanding which
modifiable factors most strongly predict stress and anxiety in students can
guide targeted campus interventions. This analysis uses cross-sectional
survey data from 1,200 university students to examine these relationships.
Variables include sleep quality, weekly study hours, GPA, anxiety scores,
stress levels, and access to counseling services. Findings from this study
aim to inform practical recommendations for both students and university
administrators.
